summary: DHH reacts to a U.S. attorney's bold statement about pursuing intellectual property thieves across international borders. He questions the implications of such extraterritorial legal reach, wondering how sovereignty and differing national laws would interact. He raises concerns about whether local sentencing limits would apply after extradition and whether the U.S. would use economic or military leverage to force compliance. The post treats U.S. IP enforcement rhetoric as a form of imperial overreach.
key_points: U.S. attorneys claim jurisdiction over IP crimes regardless of where the accused lives
Extradition for copyright infringement raises questions about conflicting sentencing standards between nations
The U.S. could use trade and diplomatic leverage to coerce smaller nations into compliance
Laws like the DMCA have no equivalent in many countries, creating jurisdictional absurdity
money_quotes: I wonder how much leverage the U.S. would have to exercise to get a suspect of copyright infringement extradited from Denmark.
Extradite or (we complicate trade|rewrite Finnish references|exclude you from our missile shield)...
key_insight: U.S. claims of borderless IP enforcement reveal how economic and military power can override national sovereignty and local legal standards.
